title: What a God Cost
subtitle: Reading One Temple Account from Roman Egypt
summary: A damaged account from Roman Egypt lists what had to be paid around a temple world: oversight of priests, enclosures of Isis, oil presses, sacred offices, linen robes for the gods, kyphi, incense, myrrh, balsam, and bread for the officiating priests. Read in sequence, the papyrus shows what it took to keep divine service going through an ordinary year.

sections.title: The First Charge
sections.body_html:
The largest figure near the top is for oversight of priests: 5,500 drachmas.1 Before robes, incense, or bread, the account places supervision. Priests had to be watched, registered, and charged for. Another papyrus from Theadelphia makes the same point from the side. In 104-105 CE, Pekysis son of Satabous paid 12 silver drachmas, plus surcharges, as admission tax of priests.10 Priestly service did not float free of taxation. Entry into sacred office could itself generate a receipt.10

The same opening section turns from people to places. Two temple enclosures at Nilopolis, one of Isis Nepherses and one of Isis Nephremmis, carry a charge of 2,100 drachmas plus surcharge.1 Then comes the village secretary’s fee, 95 drachmas plus surcharge.1 A shrine needed walls, staff, and a paper trail. This account gives all three on the same page. The gods may have occupied the center of the cult, but the first entries belong to the people who administered access to them.

sections.title: Oil and Offices
sections.body_html:
Next come the oil presses of Socnopaeus at Socnopaiou Nesos: 142 drachmas 2 1/2 obols, plus surcharge.1 The number is smaller, but the line matters because it shows a temple world tied to production as well as consumption. Oil was needed constantly for lamps. A late first-century BCE oath from Oxyrhynchus shows four lamplighters binding themselves and their property to supply the proper oil for the daily lamps in the shrines of Sarapis, Isis, and Thoeris.2 The Louvre account does not describe the lamps themselves, but once the presses appear, the burden behind them is easy to see. A god’s room had to stay lit.12

After the presses, the papyrus names offices. For the prophet, lesones, and image-bearer of Socnopaeus and Enoupis at Nilopolis: 64 drachmas plus surcharge.1 For the prophet of the very great god Suchus: 344 drachmas.1 The account does not stop to explain what each office did. It assumes that the reader knows. Other documents supply the missing edges. In 216 CE, Aurelios Pakysis described himself as priest and stolistes of the first-rank temple at Soknopaiou Nesos.6 Titles like prophet, image-bearer, and stolistes belonged to a real hierarchy of service around the gods, and this papyrus lets us watch some of that hierarchy acquire a price.16 The line is terse, but the labor behind it was not. Someone had to open the rooms, dress the image, process offerings, and keep the sequence of ritual acts from breaking down.

sections.title: Dressing the God
sections.body_html: Then the account turns from offices to things placed directly before the divine image. Three fine linen robes for the gods: 300 drachmas total.1 The line is short, but it is enough. Divine images were clothed, and clothed repeatedly. Linen had to be bought, stored, and replaced. A second-century BCE petition by Poregebthis, priest and warden of a temple of Isis, gives a harsher glimpse of what such goods could mean. After an assault, he listed among the losses and sacred valuables a linen garment worth 2,000 drachmas, together with money and bronze equipment.7 Temple cloth in Egypt was not decorative padding. It was costly property that had to exist in usable condition.7 A robe on an image sounds ceremonial. In the accounts it looks like recurring expense, vulnerable to theft, damage, and wear like any other valuable textile.

sections.title: Smoke and Scent
sections.body_html:
Below the robes come the aromatics. Kyphi and other expenses: 500 drachmas.1 Kyphi for the god Harpocrates: 96 drachmas.1 Sacrifice and incense on the birthday of the deified Augusti: 40 drachmas.1 Myrrh and balsam for the gods on three days: 60 drachmas.1

The temple interior had to smell right as well as look right. Kyphi was a compounded perfume, not a generic pinch of incense.8 Myrrh and balsam belonged to the same expensive world of imported or closely handled substances.18 A petition about the taxed trade in juniper resin shows how easily sacred needs could spill into disputes over sale and monopoly.9 Aromatics were burnable offerings, but they were also commodities moving through taxed channels.9 The account reminds us that scent had to be purchased the same way grain and linen did. A pleasing ritual atmosphere depended on supply.

sections.title: Bread Every Day
sections.body_html:
The bottom of the account is heavier than the top because it shifts from cash to grain. Baking for the officiating priests of the five tribes of the great god Socnopaeus is reckoned at one artaba of wheat daily, for 365 artabas over a year before the festival additions are counted.1 Then feast days raise the burden. Seven days here, eight there, nineteen days for the birthday of Socnopaeus, nine for the marriage of Isis Nepherses, and other blocks in which four artabas a day are needed.1

Here the account comes closest to showing the rhythm of an actual year. Priests had to eat on ordinary days, then eat more on the days when the gods required more ceremony. A temple could not survive on piety alone; it needed a grain base stable enough to carry both routine and festival.1 An account of sacred land belonging to Isis of Taposiris Aquila, with money and corn reckoned against it, shows the agrarian side of the same problem. Sacred land was measured, classified, and taxed as land because temple life had to be fed from somewhere.3 By the time the reader reaches the bottom of the Louvre papyrus, the temple has become legible as a daily consumer of bread. That is one of the hardest truths the sheet preserves. Gods could be honored only if men were fed often enough to keep serving them.

The five tribes matter here because they turn ritual into schedule. One artaba each day keeps ordinary service going. Festival blocks push the number up because more priests are present, more hands are needed, and the god’s calendar cannot be honored on ordinary rations. The rise from daily bread to feast-day surplus is where the account stops being a list and becomes a year.

sections.title: What the Papyrus Leaves Off
sections.body_html: The sheet is still only a sheet. It does not tell us who held the stylus, who checked the calculations, or whether the year had been easy or strained. It does not list every animal, field, storehouse, and vessel standing behind these charges. Other scraps help with that missing background. A mid-third-century BCE letter about the cows of Isis and Osiris shows sacred livestock kept as productive property.12 An inventory of offerings from 213-216 CE lists gold lamps, silver objects, robes, statues, and even daily oil supplied to some village shrines.11 Temples accumulated objects, animals, and land, but this account reminds us that accumulation was not the same thing as readiness. Stored wealth still had to be converted into light, cloth, scent, and bread.11112

sections.title: What a God Cost
sections.body_html:
Read in order, P.Louvre 1.4 gives a plain answer. A god cost supervision of priests, fees to scribes, enclosures that carried charges, oil and the means to secure it, named cult offices, robes, kyphi, incense, myrrh, balsam, and bread every day of the year with more bread on feast days.1

The other papers only sharpen the same point. A temple needed enough order that the lamps could burn every day, enough land and income that priests could keep eating, enough cloth and scent that the god could keep appearing as a god, and enough agreement with officials that the whole arrangement would continue to be recognized.1231011

That is what this damaged account preserves. Not theology. Not a hymn. A year of obligations, written down before they were forgotten.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 12
              Copy of an oath. We, Thonis also called Patoiphis son of Thonis and Heraclides son of Totoës, both lamplighters of the temple of Sarapis, the most great god, and of the Isis-shrine there, and Paapis son of Thonis and Petosiris son of the aforesaid Patoiphis, both lamplighters of the temple of Thoëris, the most great goddess, at Oxyrhynchus, all four swear by Caesar, god and son of a god, to Heliodorus son of Heliodorus and Helio- dorus son of Ptolemaeus, overseers of the temples in the Oxyrhynchite and Cynopolite nomes, that we will superintend the lamps of the above mentioned temples, as aforesaid, and will supply the proper oil for the daily lamps burning in the temples signified from Thoth 1 to Mesore (intercalary day ?) 5 of the present 1st year of Caesar... in accordance with what was supplied up to the 22nd which was the 7th year; and we the aforesaid are mutually sureties and all our property is security for the performance of the duties herein written. If I observe the oath may it be well with me, but if I swear falsely, the reverse. The 1st year of Caesar... Copies of the signatures of the parties, those of Paapis and Petosiris being written by proxies.

Translation: from P.Oxy. 12
              From Apollonius, lately appointed comogrammateus of the Monimou farmstead and other villages in the upper toparchy. Account of abatements of taxation for the 11th year of the Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, as follows. Abatements of which the due amounts in money and corn are reckoned here: to Gaius Julius, son of Gaius Julius Theon, formerly chief-priest and hypomnematographus, of the land in this nome sacred to Isis of Taposiris Aquila conceded in accordance with an application presented by Gaius Julius Theon the father, in accordance with the concession made to him by Gaius Tyrannius by an edict, from the account of Caesar, on condition that he should pay at the city for taxes and other charges 2 talents ... drachmae, at ... this in the holding of Demetrius son of Po... .. arurae, of which .. are in the category of land that is unsown or dry or worn away, .. in the pastures in the estates of the lord Augustus, in place of which there are planted with olives (?), reckoned up to the 20th year in the account of ..., . arurae; at Psobthis taxed at 1 artaba 1 arura, making 1 artaba, taxed at .. 21/64 arur., at Idu ... 3/64 arur., total 1 21/64 arur., at Idu ... 3/64 arur.; money- taxes, for naubion at 200 dr. of copper, for exchange (?) ..., for naubion (?) 221 (?) dr., of which 8(?) dr. ...

sources.translation:
To Aurelios Didymos strategus of the merides of Themistos and Polemon of the Arsinoite from Aurelios Pakysis son of Tesenouphis, 
priest and stolistes of the temple of the first rank of the village of Soknopaiou Nesos of the meris of Herakleides. 
 I possess a place in the house of the wife of my son Aurelios Tesenouphis in the hamlet of Pisaïs in the meris of Themistos in which I keep in storage foodstuffs for our daily use.
 Just lately my relatives went into the place because I myself was away in Alexandria and it was discovered that the foodstuffs had been pilfered. 
 The cause of the theft, as was clear, was that the crime had been committed when the place, which is on the upper floor, had been holed through the floor. 
 When those who are living there were proved to have done this through malice aforethought, they undertook in the presence of both the archephodos of the village and others to give me seven artabas of grain against the amount of the theft. 
 But since they have agreed to the undertaking but up until now have not complied with the restitution, of necessity I am making submission of a petition which I request be placed on record, 
so that my rights be secured for me against the accused, Panouphis son of Stotoetis and Pakysis son of Kanneis. Farewell.  (2nd hand) I, Aureliou (sic) Pakysis, have submitted the petition. 
 (1st hand) Year 24 of Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus Germanicus Maximus Pius Augustus, Pharmouthi 12. 
Translation with minor modifications: J. Whitehorne, BASP 40 (2003), 203-204

sources.translation:
To King Ptolemy and Queen Cleopatra ...
                         greetings. I, Peep...es, son of P...kes, Arsinoite, ... the contractor for the retailing and
                         the tax upon the ... juniper resin ..., am being wronged by (?)
                         some ibis-buriers in the nome ...
                         who, on the one hand, ... numerous burials ...
                         of those selling illegally and ...
                         of some temples (sacred things?) and distribute to others ...
                         they purchased nothing at all from the royal (?) place of sale ... until
                   now it has not happened that they are punished ...
                         and the damage to me is not inconsiderable ...
                         accuse the ibis-buriers falsely ...
                         Translation: C.A. Láda – A. Papathomas, Tyche 30 (2015) 82

sources.translation:
To [..…]esis, archiphylakitês of Berenikis Thesmophorou, and his phylakitai, from Poregebthis, priest and warden of the temple of Isis 
from the same village. On the 15th of the present month, while I was sacrificing in the temple of Isis there 
on behalf of the king and queen and their children and ancestors, totally innocent, … Perutis, son of Perutis, 
whipped (me?)… and took… and when I cried out for help, Papontos, my …, but they removed by force from the… (and) 
planted many blows on me, and wounded my shin, and beat me in my face and took off in possession of my honey and a linen garment 
worth 2000 drachmas, and a bag containing 328 bronze drachmas, and a bronze altar and a drinking vessel, 
(all of?) which I recovered from the wardens of the temple on the 15th. I am submitting a petition to you so that you may send 
the perpetrators to Meleagros, the epistatês phylakitôn, so that, once the examination of them has been held, 
they may meet with the fitting punishment and I may recover the things which I have described. For once this has happened, ….
(J. Bauschatz, Law and Enforcement, p. 72)

sources.greek: Ζήνωνι χαίρε ιν Πατῦμις Ἁραπάκτιος ἰσιονό μος. ἐπίστη ι διότι παρακαταθήκην δέδω κα τὰς βο ῦς τῆς Ἴσεως καὶ Ὀσίρεως. καλ ῶς οὖ ν ποιήσει ς, εἰ καὶ σοὶ δοκεῖ, ἣν ἄν σοι δοκῇ βοῦν βους ἐκ τούτ ων λαβεῖν, τὰς δὲ λοιπὰς ἀποδοῦναι ὅπως ἂ ν ἔχω τὸν τὸμ βίον ἐξ αὐτῶν. ἐσκέπασες ἡμᾶς κ α ὶ ἀρχῆ ς , ἔτι δὲ καὶ νῦν οὐδεὶς ἡμᾶς μὴ σκε πάσῃ ἀλλʼ ἢ σὺ οὕνεκεν τῆς Ἴσεως. οὐδενὶ γ ὰρ ἂν ἐπίστευσα ἀλλʼ ἢ σοὶ ἵνα σκεπά σῃς ἡμᾶ ς. εὐτύχει. εὐτύχει.
sources.translation: ‘To Zenon greeting from Patumis, son of Harapaktis, steward of Isis. You know that I have given to you as a deposit the cows of Isis and Osiris. Be good enough therefore, if you too agree, to take whichever one of them you please and give me back the rest in order that I may gain my living from them. You protected us in the beginning, and now again no one but you will protect us, for the sake of Isis. For I would not have put trust in anyone but you to protect us. May you prosper.’
